request.question: Dissident Irish Republican campaign includes which paramilitary group that began attacks around 2009?

title: Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group)
Irish security forces
Dissident Irish Republican campaign
Irish National Liberation Army
United Ireland
Timeline of Continuity IRA actions
Real Irish Republican Army
Timeline of Real Irish Republican Army actions
Provisional Irish Republican Army
Official Irish Republican Army
sentences: Óglaigh na hÉireann (ONH; ] ) is the title taken by a small dissident Irish republican paramilitary group that has taken part in the dissident Irish republican campaign.
The organisation started carrying out attacks around 2009 and was formed after a split within the Real IRA.
Irish security forces is a term used to describe all security forces of the Republic of Ireland.

The term was widely used to refer to the various security operations undertaken by the Irish state during The Troubles, and still continues to be used to describe security operations aimed at undermining the ongoing dissident Irish Republican campaign.

Since the Provisional Irish Republican Army (IRA) called a ceasefire and ended its armed campaign in 1997, breakaway groups opposed to the ceasefire ("dissident Irish republicans") have continued a low-level armed campaign against the British security forces in Northern Ireland.
The main paramilitaries involved are the Real IRA, Continuity IRA and Óglaigh na hÉireann.
They have targeted the British Army and Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI, successor of the Royal Ulster Constabulary) in gun and bomb attacks, as well as with mortars and rockets.
They have also carried out bombings that are meant to cause disruption.
However, their campaign has not been as intensive as the Provisional IRA's.
The Irish National Liberation Army (INLA, Irish: "Arm Saoirse Náisiúnta na hÉireann" ) is an Irish republican socialist paramilitary group formed in December 1974, during "the Troubles".
It seeks to remove Northern Ireland from the United Kingdom and create a socialist republic encompassing all of Ireland.
It is the paramilitary wing of the Irish Republican Socialist Party (IRSP).
United Ireland is the proposition that the whole of Ireland should be a single sovereign state.
At present, the island is divided politically; the sovereign state of Ireland has jurisdiction over the majority of Ireland, while Northern Ireland is part of the United Kingdom.
Achieving a united Ireland is a central tenet of Irish nationalism, particularly of both mainstream and dissident Irish republican political and paramilitary organisations.
Unionists support Northern Ireland remaining part of the United Kingdom, and therefore oppose Irish unification.
This is a chronology of activities by the Continuity Irish Republican Army (CIRA), an Irish republican paramilitary group.
The group started operations in 1994, after the Provisional Irish Republican Army began a ceasefire.
The Real Irish Republican Army or Real IRA (RIRA), is a dissident Irish republican paramilitary organisation which aims to bring about a united Ireland.
It formed in 1997 following a split in the Provisional IRA by dissident members, who rejected the IRA's ceasefire that year.

Like the Provisional IRA before it, the RIRA sees itself as the only rightful successor to the original Irish Republican Army and styles itself as "the Real Irish Republican Army" in English or "Óglaigh na hÉireann" in Irish.
It is an illegal organisation in the Republic of Ireland and designated as proscribed terrorist organisation in the United Kingdom and the United States.
This is a timeline of actions by the Real Irish Republican Army, also called the Real IRA, an Irish republican paramilitary group.
The group was formed in late 1997 by members of the Provisional Irish Republican Army who disagreed with that organisation's ceasefire.

The Provisional Irish Republican Army (Provisional IRA) was an Irish republican paramilitary organization that sought to remove Northern Ireland from the United Kingdom and to bring about an independent socialist republic encompassing all of Ireland.
It was the biggest and most active republican paramilitary group during the Troubles.
It saw itself as the successor to the original IRA and called itself simply the Irish Republican Army (IRA), or "Óglaigh na hÉireann" in Irish, and was widely referred to as such by others.

The Official Irish Republican Army or Official IRA (OIRA) was an Irish republican paramilitary group whose goal was to remove Northern Ireland from the United Kingdom and create a "workers' republic" encompassing all of Ireland.
It emerged in December 1969, shortly after the beginning of the Troubles, when the Irish Republican Army split into two factions.
The other was the Provisional IRA.
Each continued to call itself simply "the IRA" and rejected the other's legitimacy.
Unlike the "Provisionals", the "Officials" were Marxist and worked to form a united front with other Irish communist groups, named the Irish National Liberation Front (NLF).
The Officials were called the NLF by the Provisionals and were sometimes nicknamed the "Red IRA" by others.

title: Dissident Irish Republican campaign
Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group)
Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group)

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Dissident Irish Republican campaign includes which paramilitary group that began attacks around 2009?
hop1_docs: Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group) | Óglaigh na hÉireann (ONH; ] ) is the title taken by a small dissident Irish republican paramilitary group that has taken part in the dissident Irish republican campaign.  The organisation started carrying out attacks around 2009 and was formed after a split within the Real IRA.

Dissident Irish Republican campaign | Since the Provisional Irish Republican Army (IRA) called a ceasefire and ended its armed campaign in 1997, breakaway groups opposed to the ceasefire ("dissident Irish republicans") have continued a low-level armed campaign against the British security forces in Northern Ireland.  The main paramilitaries involved are the Real IRA, Continuity IRA and Óglaigh na hÉireann.  They have targeted the British Army and Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI, successor of the Royal Ulster Constabulary) in gun and bomb attacks, as well as with mortars and rockets.  They have also carried out bombings that are meant to cause disruption.  However, their campaign has not been as intensive as the Provisional IRA's.

Dissident republican | Dissident republicans, renegade republicans, anti-Agreement republicans or anti-ceasefire republicans (Irish: "poblachtach easaontach" ) are Irish republicans who do not support the current peace agreements in Northern Ireland.  The agreements followed a 30-year conflict known as the Troubles, which claimed over 3,500 lives.  During the conflict, republican paramilitary groups such as the Provisional Irish Republican Army waged a campaign to bring about a united Irish republic.  Peace negotiations in the 1990s led to an IRA ceasefire in 1994 and to the Good Friday Agreement of 1998.  Mainstream republicans, represented by Sinn Féin, supported the Agreement as a means of achieving Irish unity peacefully.  'Dissidents' saw this as an abandonment of republican ideals and acceptance of partition and British rule.  They hold that the Northern Ireland Assembly and Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) are illegitimate and see the PSNI as a "British paramilitary police force".

Real Irish Republican Army | The Real Irish Republican Army or Real IRA (RIRA), is a dissident Irish republican paramilitary organisation which aims to bring about a united Ireland.  It formed in 1997 following a split in the Provisional IRA by dissident members, who rejected the IRA's ceasefire that year.  Like the Provisional IRA before it, the RIRA sees itself as the only rightful successor to the original Irish Republican Army and styles itself as "the Real Irish Republican Army" in English or "Óglaigh na hÉireann" in Irish.  It is an illegal organisation in the Republic of Ireland and designated as proscribed terrorist organisation in the United Kingdom and the United States.

Timeline of Continuity IRA actions | This is a chronology of activities by the Continuity Irish Republican Army (CIRA), an Irish republican paramilitary group.  The group started operations in 1994, after the Provisional Irish Republican Army began a ceasefire.

Irish Republican Voice | Irish Republican Voice was a political group founded by dissident republicans.  The group was believed to be the political wing of the group Saoirse na hEireann (itself an RIRA splinter group).  The IRV have denied this though they say they recognise it as the only legitimate army.  Members include prominent dissident republicans such as Paul Stewart and James McDonagh.

Fenian dynamite campaign | The Fenian dynamite campaign (or Fenian bombing campaign) was a paramilitary campaign orchestrated by Irish republicans against the British Empire, between the years 1881 and 1885.  The campaign was associated with Fenianism; that is to say the Irish revolutionary organisations which aimed to establish an independent Irish Republic; such as the Irish Republican Brotherhood, the Fenian Brotherhood, Clan na Gael and the United Irishmen of America.  The campaign, led by Jeremiah O'Donovan Rossa and other Irishmen exiled in the United States, was a form of asymmetrical warfare and targeted infrastructure, government, military and police targets in Great Britain (particularly London).  The campaign led to the establishment of secret police group Special Branch (originally known as the Special Irish Branch).
hop1_titles: Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group)
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Óglaigh na hÉireann — [1]: "has taken part in the dissident Irish republican campaign."
- Óglaigh na hÉireann — [1]: "started carrying out attacks around 2009"
- Dissident Irish Republican campaign — [2]: "The main paramilitaries involved are the Real IRA, Continuity IRA and Óglaigh na hÉireann."
- Real IRA — [4]: "formed in 1997"
- Continuity IRA (CIRA) — [5]: "The group started operations in 1994"

Ambiguities:
- "Óglaigh na hÉireann" label is used for multiple pages: ONH splinter group (passage [1]) and Real IRA styles itself as "Óglaigh na hÉireann" (passage [4]).

Bridge / missing fact:
- The passages do provide the needed relation: which dissident group began attacks around 2009 — Óglaigh na hÉireann (the ONH splinter) is explicitly stated to have "started carrying out attacks around 2009" and "has taken part in the dissident Irish republican campaign." Final answer: Óglaigh na hÉireann (ONH).

Retrieval direction:
- Open page: "Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group)"
- Open page: "Óglaigh na hÉireann" (disambiguation) or search: "Óglaigh na hÉireann ONH 2009 attacks"
- Open page: "Dissident Irish Republican campaign"
- Open page: "Real Irish Republican Army"
hop2_docs: Óglaigh na hÉireann (Real IRA splinter group) | Óglaigh na hÉireann (ONH; ] ) is the title taken by a small dissident Irish republican paramilitary group that has taken part in the dissident Irish republican campaign.  The organisation started carrying out attacks around 2009 and was formed after a split within the Real IRA.

Óglaigh na hÉireann | Óglaigh na hÉireann (] ), abbreviated ÓÉ, is an Irish-language idiom that can be translated variously as "soldiers of Ireland", "warriors of Ireland", "volunteers of Ireland" or "Irish volunteers".  In traditional Gaelic script, it is written Óglaıġ na hÉıreann.

Organisation of National Ex-Servicemen | The Organisation of National Ex-Servicemen and Women (Irish: "Óglaigh Náisiúnta Na hÉireann" ) is a support organisation for ex-service personnel of the Irish Defence Services.

Defence Forces (Ireland) | The military of Ireland, known as the Defence Forces (Irish: "Fórsaí Cosanta" , officially styled "Óglaigh na hÉireann" ), encompass the Army, Air Corps, Naval Service and Reserve Defence Forces.

Irish Republican Army (1922–69) | The original Irish Republican Army (IRA) fought a guerrilla war against British rule in Ireland in the Irish War of Independence between 1919 and 1921.  Following the signing of the Anglo-Irish Treaty on 6 December 1921, the IRA in the 26 counties that were to become the Irish Free State split between supporters and opponents of the Treaty.  The anti-Treatyites, sometimes referred to by Free State forces as Irregulars, continued to use the name Irish Republican Army (IRA) or in Irish Óglaigh na hÉireann, as did the organisation in Northern Ireland which originally supported the pro-Treaty side (if not the Treaty). " Óglaigh na hÉireann" was also adopted as the name of the pro-Treaty National Army, and remains the official legal title of the Irish Defence Forces.  This article deals with the anti-Treaty IRA that fought against the Irish Free State in the Irish Civil War, and with its successors up to 1969, when the IRA split again.

List of Irish Republican Army chiefs of staff | The following is the list of those who are reported to have served as Chief of Staff of the Irish Republican Army (Irish: "Ceann Foirne Óglaigh na hÉireann" ) in the organisations bearing that name.  Due to the clandestine nature of these organisations, this list is not definitive.

Murder of Andrew Burns | Andrew Burns (c. 1980 – 12 February 2008) was a Northern Irish man and a member of the Real IRA, murdered by Óglaigh na hÉireann.  According to "The Guardian", at the time of his death, Burns was believed to have been, "the first victim of a Republican terrorist murder in Northern Ireland for six years."
